data.title: Vacasa Determines Davidson Kempner Bid Doesn't Surpass Casago Offer
data.description:
By Denny Jacob

Vacasa said the special committee of its board determined that the unsolicited bid from Davidson Kempner Capital Management isn't a superior offer to the agreement between Vacasa and Casago.

The vacation rental company said the committee determined a hitch in Davidson's proposal was the fact that it remained conditioned upon receipt of an amendment to its tax-receivable agreement and subject to materially greater risks regarding closing certainty than the Casago transaction, particularly in light of recent market volatility and uncertainty.

Vacasa in March entered into an amended agreement in which Casago will acquire all outstanding shares of the company held by public shareholders at a price of $5.30 a share.

Write to Denny Jacob at denny.jacob@wsj.com

data.title: Mercantile Bank Corp expected to post earnings of $1.19 a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Mercantile Bank Corp is expected to show a rise in quarterly revenue when it reports results on April 22 for the period ending March 31 2025

  • The Grand Rapids Michigan-based company is expected to report a 14.7% increase in revenue to $57.43 million from $50.07 million a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 4 analysts, based on LSEG data.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Mercantile Bank Corp is for earnings of $1.19 per share.

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," 3 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Mercantile Bank Corp is $52.50​, above​ its last closing price of $40.68. ​​​

data.title: Focused Impact: Cascale's Path to 2030
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / April 18, 2025 / In our latest blog post, Cascale CEO Colin Browne unveils our refined strategy for accelerating collective action across the consumer goods industry. He shares key insights into our renewed focus on driving measurable impact through two strategic pillars: tackling climate change and supporting decent work for all. From the Industry Decarbonization Roadmap to Better Buying, Browne outlines how Cascale is aligning its members around shared priorities, practical tools, and measurable outcomes to create lasting change.

Read the full blog, titled: Focused Impact: Cascale's Path to 2030

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Cascale on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Cascale

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/cascale

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Cascale

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. BETH VAN DUYNE (R-TX)
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 18, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

REP. BETH VAN DUYNE (R-TX)

FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. BETH VAN DUYNE

(R-TX)

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. BETH VAN DUYNE

(R-TX)

APRIL 18, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. BETH VAN DUYNE (R-TX)

MARIA BARTIROMO, FBN ANCHOR

BARTIROMO: Joining me now is Texas Congresswoman Beth Van Duyne. Congresswoman, thanks very much for being here. Do you support that plan?

REP. BETH VAN DUYNE (R-TX): Look, I think we have everything on the table, but I don't support raising taxes. I think we don't have — we do not have a revenue generating problem in the U.S. We have a spending problem in the U.S. You are not going to be able to tax your way or grow your way out of it. We need to get control of our spending. And I think we have an opportunity to do that. You know, every year we have our budgets that come in, and instead of just, you know, making them come from a base budget and build their budget, we just look at what they spent last year. We try to shave it. It's not going to work.

BARTIROMO: Right.

DUYNE: So I am all for it. I've been on Ways and Means. We've been working on this tax bill for two — over two years now, but we have got to get control of government spend and not even think about increasing taxes.

BARTIROMO: Well, I mean, you're right, because when you look at the COVID numbers, COVID spending went off the charts. And you know, rather than going back the year before, why don't you just go back to 2019 and start looking at those numbers, because raising taxes even on just the highest earners would go completely against what President Trump has been campaigning on for the last several years.

DUYNE: I agree with the 100 percent and I've been pushing for pre-COVID spending. This is why, we went to — had the limit save grow bill, Limit Save Grow Act a couple of years ago, we are pushing for pre-COVID spending. It's one thing to do during a pandemic. We have got to get back to that level, and again, we need to start talking about cutting, not shaving.

BARTIROMO: OK. Meanwhile, Maryland Senator Chris Van Hollen met and shook hands with the alleged MS-13 gang member, Kilmar Abrego Garcia in El Salvador. The senator traveled to El Salvador on Wednesday. He's been pushing to bring Garcia back to Maryland after the Supreme Court ordered the U.S. to facilitate his release. The Trump administration says Garcia was wrongly deported due to administrative error. Van Hollen writes this on X. I said my main goal of this trip was to meet with Kilmar. Tonight, I had the chance, I have called his wife Jennifer to pass along his message of love. I look forward to providing a full update upon my return.

Then there's Arizona Congresswoman Yassamin Ansari says she's also headed to El Salvador. She says, quote, our rights shouldn't be revoked to propagate Trump's authoritarian agenda. My parents fled in an authoritarian regime in Iran, where people disappeared. I refuse to sit back and watch it happen here too. Three other House Democrats are requesting a congressional delegation to El Salvador, Congresswoman, your thoughts.

DUYNE: Well, I hope to take a 757 during votes and take them all down there, and they can go ahead, and they can try to get more folks who have beat their wives, who have been stopped for human trafficking. They're picking the wrong fights. You've got an American citizen right now who is being held by Hamas in Gaza. Where is their outrage there? I think they're picking the wrong fights.

We have got to work on the 20 million people who have entered our country illegally over the last four years. You got Tom Homan, you got Kristi Noem, who are doing their job. They are enforcing the laws, and when we've got sanctuary city policies that are enabling these people to stay, they're committing crimes, I want to see their outrage for the victimsaEURO(TM) families who have been murdered, who have been raped as a direct result of illegal immigration.

Instead, what they're doing is they are protecting a person who came here illegally, who has been accused of beating his wife, and that's the stake that they're willing to die on. If that's the hill they want to go on. God bless them.

BARTIROMO: So, I mean, look, it was a mistake. The administration said it was a mistake.

DUYNE: It might have been a mistake, but they're picking the one straw out of an entire barn full. And what we need to focus on is the 20 million that we're allowed to enter here, people on the terrorist watchlist, people from drug cartels.

BARTIROMO: Yes.

DUYNE: And that's what this administration, you can pull one example out. But if that is literally the person that they're going to hail as their savior for this, I think it's a mistake.

BARTIROMO: Yes, it's incredible actually, Congresswoman, you've got a new bill which mandates small business owners prove that they and their employees are U.S. citizens in order to receive small business loans. Tell us more about that. How many illegal nationals are receiving loans?

DUYNE: Well, we don't know, and that's part of the problem. We've seen money that was going out during COVID. We've seen money that's been going out overseas to people who never should have gotten it illegally, and those are billions of dollars that could have been spent primarily to help our small businesses. When we start thinking about, you know, the tax bill that we're putting together, we want to encourage growth. We want investment in the U.S. We want manufacturing to come back on shore. That is the whole purpose of the tax bill.

And this bill basically is trying to codify what this administration is doing is people who are getting money illegally should — that should be stopped, and we're going to try to prevent it from being taken away from us again under a new administration by codifying and putting it into law.

BARTIROMO: And was this by design under the Biden administration, just to give illegal people here in America as much support as they could, or an oversight? How does this happen where American citizens who need small business loans are getting shut out because there's not enough to go around, because illegal are getting them.

DUYNE: Yes, don't ask me to justify the Biden administration's policies. I can't. I was on small business for last five years, and I can tell you that there were billions of dollars that were sent out to people that who never should have gotten them. And instead of trying to follow up, the last administration — the last administrator didn't even do that.

So I think this administration, you know, Kelly Loeffler is doing a great job of saying, no more. We're going to put an end to that, and we're going to try to clean up the SBA and make sure that people who should be getting those dollars are small businesses. Should be benefiting. Those are the people who are going to be getting those dollars.

BARTIROMO: Yes, it's a good point to make. Congresswoman, where are you in terms of the reconciliation package when you and your colleagues get back from the Easter recess, it's got to be all-hands-on-deck, because Mike Johnson wants that bill on the President's desk by the end of May.

DUYNE: Yes, we have been working on, as I said, for the last two and a half years on Ways and Means. We've had field hearings across the country to get the input of job creators, of employees. We have seen the benefits of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and what they were able to do, how they're able to invest in their employees, the future growth of their companies, offer health care for the first time, all these benefits that they got out of the TCJ and we've got to prevent that from expiring at the end of the year. We have been very busy. We're going over the tax policy. We spent hours now in the Library of Congress and Ways and Means going line-by-line.

And we are going to make that that deadline and make sure that we don't see the highest increase in taxes on 90 percent of American taxpayers.

BARTIROMO: All right, we will be watching your work, Congresswoman. It's good to see you this morning. Thanks so much. Beth Van Duyne joining us.

END

data.title: Dynex Capital Inc expected to post earnings of 22 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Dynex Capital Inc , is expected to show a rise in quarterly revenue when it reports results on April 21 for the period ending March 31 2025

data.title: Building Electrolyzers in Guadalajara, Spain
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / April 18, 2025 / Cummins Inc

Employees working on electrolyzers inside the Guadalajara manufacturing plant | Photos by Accelera™ by Cummins

Cummins

David Heredero, Guadalajara Plant Director for Electrolyzers at AcceleraTM by Cummins shares his thoughts:

Q: What is your team working on?

David Heredero: My team is working on defining the best approach to building electrolyzers in our plant in Guadalajara, setting up the right processes, the right organization, and a performance-driven culture.

Q: What do you love about your job?

David Heredero: I love the great opportunity to contribute to shaping this new industry and to do it together with people who bring unique backgrounds and from whom it is great to learn.

Q: What have you learned from your team?

David Heredero: Their passion, excitement and determination to make great things happen have been valuable lessons from my team. They are a strong group of people, ready to overcome difficulties and changes.

Q: What impact do you hope this work will have?

David Heredero: We will play a key role in the energy transition. The more electrolyzers we produce from our plant in Guadalajara, the more we will help decarbonize the economy.

Accelera, the zero-emissions business segment of Cummins Inc., opened its new electrolyzer manufacturing plant in Guadalajara, Castilla-La Mancha, Spain, in October 2024.

Spanning 24,000 square meters (~260,000 square feet), the facility marks a €50 million investment and is one of the world's largest dedicated to electrolyzer production. With an initial production capacity of 500 megawatts (MW) of proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzers annually, the plant is designed to scale beyond 1 gigawatt (GW) as demand grows. The facility currently employs around 150 highly skilled workers, with the potential to reach 200 as operations ramp up.

In line with Accelera's commitment to sustainability, the facility integrates 3,000 rooftop solar panels, a geothermal heating and cooling system, and rainwater harvesting, enabling up to 70% of its energy needs to be met on-site. This plant plays an important role in advancing Europe's green hydrogen production.

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Cummins Inc on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Cummins Inc

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/cummins-inc

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Cummins Inc

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: A Greener Partnership
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / April 18, 2025 / Henkel has deepened its commitment to sustainability through an ongoing partnership with the Mill River Park Collaborative in Stamford, CT

Henkel continues to deepen its commitment to environmental stewardship through an ongoing partnership with the Mill River Park Collaborative in Stamford, CT. This engaging partnership sees Henkel employees actively participating in sustainability initiatives and park revitalization projects, fostering a stronger connection between the company and the local community.

Since 2021, Henkel and Mill River Park Collaborative, the nonprofit dedicated to creating and sustaining the park, have partnered to provide children with programming through Henkel Researchers' World, a global education initiative designed to ignite a passion for science. To date, Henkel has reached over 7,000 children in the greater Stamford area through its dedicated classroom in the Whittingham Discovery Center in Mill River Park, including 4th grade classes in the city.

In addition to Henkel Researchers World, the company's support of the park encompasses employee volunteerism and other programs throughout the year. Most recently, Henkel announced the establishment of the Henkel Welcoming Garden at the park, a place to gather, relax, play, learn and connect with nature.

"Henkel employees regularly enjoy volunteer activities such as planting bulbs or park cleanup and beautification, and bring their friends and family to enjoy the beautiful spaces and exciting activities that the park has to offer," said Phil Schaffer, Corporate Senior Vice President Henkel Consumer Brands, North America. "Henkel is a proud member of the Stamford community, and we appreciate the opportunity to show support and gratitude to our local partners and neighbors now and for many years to come."

Watch the video to learn more about Henkel and Mill River Park Collaborative's partnership

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Henkel on 3blmedia.com.

data.title: China, Cambodia vow supply chain cooperation, sign canal deal
data.description:
China and Cambodia have agreed to build safe and stable supply chains together and to strengthen cooperation in transportation infrastructure, they said in a joint statement released by China's foreign ministry on Friday.

The two countries also signed a deal on the construction of a major canal Cambodia hopes will transform its economic fortunes, though the investment and scale of the project have been apparently trimmed.

The agreements came at the conclusion of Chinese President Xi Jinping's three-nation tour of Southeast Asia that included stops in Vietnam and Malaysia.

The trip was part of Beijing's effort to consolidate economic and trading ties with close neighbours in the face of China's tariff standoff with the United States.

"China supports Cambodia in building the Funan Techo Integrated Water Conservancy Project in accordance with the principles of feasibility and sustainability," the joint statement said.

The canal project, which was previously estimated to cost $1.7 billion - nearly 4% of the country's annual gross domestic product - and stretching 180 km (112 miles), is now valued at $1.16 billion with a length of 151.6 km, the Cambodian government said in a separate statement on Friday.

It will be financed through a public-private partnership, the statement showed, with Cambodian investors holding a 51% stake and Chinese investors holding 49%.

China also commended Cambodia's efforts in cracking down on illegal online gambling and telecom fraud in the joint statement, with the two countries agreeing to further strengthen law enforcement cooperation.

Before Xi's visit, the Cambodian government said it had deported to China a number of "Chinese criminals", including people from Taiwan, in a move that angered Taipei and was praised by Beijing.

The two countries also said they had agreed to establish ministerial dialogue between their foreign and defence ministers to facilitate coordination on major strategic issues.

data.title: Dollar Tree (DLTR) Soars 8% After CFO Loads Up Thousands of Shares
data.description:
Dollar Tree shares surged 8% on Thursday, boosted by insider buying and a growing preference for discount shopping amid persistent inflation.

Chief Financial Officer Stewart Glendinning acquired 17,000 shares on April 14 and April 15 at prices ranging from $72.50 to $73.00, spending about US$1.24 million. The transactions increased his direct holdings in the retailer to 49,353 shares.

Meanwhile, Chief Accounting Officer Aditya Maheshwari sold 1,238 shares on April 14 at $74.705 apiece, for a total of roughly US$92,500. He now directly owns 4,231 shares.

Rising consumer prices continue to push shoppers toward discount chains. Dollar Tree's low-cost model and consistent foot traffic have helped it gain market share while mid-tier competitors struggle with pricing sensitivity.

Analysts say tariffs are also playing in the company's favor. While many retailers face pressure from import costs, Dollar Tree's bulk purchasing strategy allows it to manage expenses and maintain pricing power.

data.title: Japan Negotiator Akazawa: U.S. Trade Policies Raising Risk of Downturn
data.description:
By Peter Landers

Japan's economy faces downward pressure because of U.S. tariffs, said the minister in charge of negotiating with the Trump administration on trade.

"Because of the effects of U.S. trade policy, the risk of an economic downturn is rising," said Ryosei Akazawa, the minister for economic revitalization in Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba's cabinet and the person named by Ishiba to lead trade negotiations with Washington.

He spoke at a news conference Friday after the government downgraded its assessment of the economy in its monthly report, citing Trump's tariffs.

Akazawa met President Trump at the White House on Wednesday for 50 minutes and afterwards held trade negotiations with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and other top U.S. officials for 75 minutes.

Trump said afterward that the talks made "big progress," without giving details.

On Friday, Akazawa said he was able to get a general grasp of the themes the U.S. is interested in but wouldn't go so far as to say that the stage is set for a deal. He said Japan hoped to resume cabinet-level negotiations with the U.S. this month and make concrete progress.

The Ishiba government is facing domestic pressure to get tougher with the U.S. On Wednesday, opposition lawmaker Shinji Oguma told Foreign Minister Takeshi Iwaya at a parliamentary committee hearing that the president's tactics were like a "stickup by a juvenile delinquent" and "if you give him money, he'll come back to stick you up again." Oguma said Japan should team up with other nations to fight the U.S. demands.

Iwaya didn't respond to the characterization of Trump but said Japan would firmly defend its interests and work with other nations. Akazawa reiterated Friday that Japan was seeking the complete elimination of tariffs Trump has imposed on Japanese goods, including a 10% general tariff and 25% tariffs on autos and steel. Those tariffs also apply to other nations.

Write to Peter Landers at peter.landers@wsj.com

data.title: Is the stock market open today? Are banks closed?
data.description:
By Charles Passy

And don't forget bond markets, either. Here's what you need to know.

The financial world is taking a Good Friday pause.

The two major stock exchanges - the New York Stock Exchange and the Nasdaq - will be closed on Friday in observance of the Christian holiday. The same goes for bond markets, which also closed early on Thursday.

This is a rare instance when markets shut down on a day that is not a federal holiday. So even if you can't trade stocks on the U.S. exchanges, you can still expect to receive your mail - the U.S. Postal Service is open for business as usual. Banks, which typically follow the federal calendar, will generally be open as well.

Why are markets SPX COMP DJIA closed? As a post on the Nasdaq website explains, the shutdown is "largely due to historical tradition rather than any direct religious mandate. ... This practice has been in place since at least the late 19th century."

There are some other holiday shutdowns to note. Many schools are closed as part of a spring break. FedEx (FDX) is operating with slightly modified service, though UPS (UPS) is maintaining its regular service.

Easter Monday is also a holiday in much of the world, with many financial markets, most notably in Europe, shuttered until Tuesday.

-Charles Passy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
